segments.s387-g121: بیفگند is an epic/past form of افکندن (to throw) here in the third-person singular perfective — “he threw/ cast.” زیشان is a fused prepositional/object phrase (زِ ایشان) meaning “of them / many of them,” followed by فراوان as a quantifier meaning “abundant / many.” The locative/directional phrase به راه (به + راه) follows the object and marks direction (“onto/to the road”). The subject is omitted and supplied from context (typical in Persian).
segments.s387-g122: وزان is a contracted sequence (و + زان = و از آن) meaning “and from that/there.” جایگه is an archaic poetic form of جایگاه meaning “place/spot.” رفت is the simple past third-person singular of رفتن (he went). نزدیک شاه is an adverbial/prepositional phrase “near the king” describing the destination or proximity; word order (verb then place) expresses movement toward the king.
segments.s387-g17: گر is the conditional particle 'if.' ایدونک is an archaic/poetic adverbial particle meaning 'ever'/'at any time.' پیروز گردم is a verb phrase: پیروز (victorious) + گردم (from گشتن ‘to become’) in a subjunctive/optative sense, so here 'should I become victorious.' به جنگ is a prepositional phrase ('in/at war') modifying the becoming.
segments.s387-g18: کنم is the first-person singular present/subjunctive of کردن used here as the main verb 'I will make/do.' روی گیتی is a nominative phrase 'the face (روی) of the world (گیتی)' where روی precedes its head; بر is the preposition with the following name meaning 'against' or 'upon,' and تنگ is an adjective 'narrow/closed' used predicatively: literally 'I will make the world's face narrow against Arjasp' (i.e. make the world hostile to Arjasp). Note the omitted object marker را, typical in poetry.
segments.s387-g19: بخواهیم is first-person plural present/subjunctive/optative 'let us wish/let us demand' (an exhortation or plan). ازو ('از او') is the older poetic form 'from him.' کین is the noun 'vengeance' acting as the thing to be demanded, and لهراسپ شاه is a genitive apposition 'Lohrasp, the king' specifying whose vengeance is meant.
segments.s387-g20: همان 'that same' repeats/identifies the specific کین (vengeance) just mentioned. چندین سر بیگناه is a quantified noun phrase 'many heads (سر) innocent (بیگناه)' where چندین precedes the counted noun and بیگناه follows as a postpositive adjective. The clause omits an explicit object marker or verb linking (ellipsis common in epic poetry), so this phrase supplies the content of the vengeance to be exacted.
segments.s387-g21: برادر here functions collectively as 'brothers.' جهان‌بین is an epithet 'world-seeing' or 'world-wise' modifying برادر. من is the possessive 'my' attached after the noun in the older/poetic word order, and سی و هشت gives the number 'thirty-eight' that qualifies برادر (the numeral follows the noun, a normal pattern in classical Persian poetry).
segments.s387-g22: که is a relative pronoun 'who/whose' linking to those brothers. از خونشان is a prepositional phrase 'from their blood' (شان = their). لعل شد is a causative/result phrase: لعل 'ruby' + شد 'became' — 'became ruby' — and خاک دشت 'the soil of the plain' is the subject that became ruby (word order here is poetic: 'from their blood the plain's soil became ruby').
segments.s387-g23: پذیرفتم is the simple past first-person 'I accepted/took (upon myself).' از + داور دادگر indicates the source/agent 'from the just judge' — داور دادگر is a compound epithet 'the just arbiter.' The verb is perfective/past and introduces the accepted ruling or decree.
segments.s387-g24: که introduces the content clause of what was accepted: کینه نگیرم is negative subjunctive/present-jussive 'that I not take vengeance' (نگیرم: negative + subjunctive ending -م for first person). ز is the poetic preposition 'from/on account of,' بند here means 'fetter/imprisonment' and پدر 'father' completes the genitive: 'on account of my father's imprisonment.' The clause expresses a pledged restraint.
segments.s387-g1: چو شب شد is a temporal clause: the first چو here means "when" and شد is the past of شو/شد "to become/fall (i.e. 'fell' as in 'night fell')". The second چو introduces a simile "like/as" (چو آهرمن کینه‌خواه = "like Ahriman, the vengeful one"); کینه‌خواه is an epithet attached to آهرمن.
segments.s387-g2: خروش جرس خاست is the main clause: خاست is an older/past form meaning "arose/began" (cf. برخاست). خروش جرس = "the sound/peal of the bell" (noun + noun in Persian), and از بارگاه is a simple prepositional phrase "from the palace/court."
segments.s387-g3: بران باره is a fixed adverbial phrase meaning "on that occasion / thereupon" (بران = "upon that", باره = "occasion"). پهلوی برنشست is a verb phrase: پهلوی ("at the side") + برنشست (an older past form meaning "sat down / took a seat"), so together "sat down at (his) side."
segments.s387-g4: یکی is an indefinite "one/a certain person"; تیغ هندی is a noun (تیغ, "sword") modified by an adjective/attributive هندی ("Indian"). گرفته is a past-participle/secondary predicate meaning "having taken" or "seized," and به دست is the prepositional phrase "into/into the hand" (i.e. "in his hand"). The whole phrase describes someone who sat with an Indian sword in hand.
segments.s387-g5: چو at the start is the temporal 'when.' The three following items are coordinated names (نوشاذر، بهمن، مهرنوش) joined by و; they function as the subject(s) of the following verb. The repeated و is the normal conjunction 'and.'
segments.s387-g6: برفتند is the past plural verb "they went/they departed." یکسر is an adverb meaning "entirely/at once." پر از is the adjective phrase "full of" (پر + از), followed by the nouns جنگ و جوش: جنگ (here in the sense of battle/war) and جوش (here meaning zeal/ardor), coordinated by و.
segments.s387-g7: ورا is an unstressed pronominal/adverbial meaning "for him/to him" (marks beneficiary or direction). راهبر is the noun "guide/leader" and functions as the subject; پیش جاماسپ بود literally "was before Jamasp," where پیش means "in the presence of / before" and بود is the past copula "was." The overall ordering (avra subject prepositional phrase copula) is typical of older/epic Persian.
segments.s387-g8: که is a relative pronoun "who/which/that" linking to راهبر; دستور here means an office/title (dastur), i.e. a minister or chief officer, rather than simply 'order.' فرخنده is an epithet "auspicious/fortunate" modifying گشتاسپ (Gushtasp), and بود is the past copula "was": "who was the minister of the auspicious Gushtasp."
segments.s387-g105: ببردی is a past 2nd-person singular verb ('you took away'). ازین is the prepositional phrase 'از + این' (here written together) meaning 'from this'. پادشاهی is a genitive/possessive noun 'kingdom/kingship' and فروغ is the thing taken: 'the splendor/glory'. Word order here places the prepositional source (ازین پادشاهی) before the affected noun فروغ: 'you took the splendor from this kingdom.'
segments.s387-g106: همی is a classical/narrative marker with a progressive/emphatic force ('then/was in the act of' / 'still'); چاره جستی is a 2nd-person past ('you sought a remedy/means') where جستی is the past of jostan 'to seek'. بگفت is a past 3rd-person ('he/it said' or 'he told') and دروغ is its object ('a lie' / 'falsehood'). The clause links as a contrastive remark after the first clause: 'you sought a remedy, (but) he uttered a lie.'
segments.s387-g107: بدین is the prepositional phrase 'به + این' (classical contraction) meaning 'in/with this' or 'by this'. رزم خونی is a noun phrase 'bloody battle' (رزم = battle, خونی = bloody/of blood). که شد ریخته is a relative clause: که ('that/which') + شد as an auxiliary/past marker + ریخته (past participle 'poured/spilled'), yielding 'in which blood was spilled' or 'that has been stained with blood.'
segments.s387-g108: تو باشی uses باشی (here a narrative/present form used for 'you are' / 'you remain'); تو is the 2nd-person pronoun. بدان (ب + دان in the tokenization) functions as 'به + آن' = 'to/onto that' (i.e., 'upon that'), linking to گیتی آویخته ('hung upon the world'). گیتی آویخته is a predicate adjective phrase: گیتی 'the world' and آویخته 'hung/attached' — 'you are hung upon that world' (i.e., your fate is bound to it).
segments.s387-g109: وزان is a compound prepositional/connecting form 'و + زَ/از + آن' meaning 'and from that' or 'from that place/then'. دشت گریـان is a noun phrase 'the weeping plain' (دشت = plain, گریـان = weeping/weepful). سراندر is an adverbial/complement meaning 'into the interior / inward', and کشید is a past 3rd-person verb 'drew / pulled / moved' (here 'moved/drew inward'). Together: 'From that weeping plain he drew inward.'
segments.s387-g110: به is the preposition 'toward/into/at'. انبوه modifies گردان ('a dense/massed troop'), so انبوه گردان ترکان is 'the numerous/dense troop(s) of the Turks.' رسید is the past 3rd-person verb 'arrived / reached.' The phrase means 'he reached the massed ranks of the Turks.'
segments.s387-g111: سپه دید is a simple past clause 'the army saw' (سپه = army, دید = saw). بر هفت فرسنگ دشت expresses spatial extent: بر = 'over/on/across', هفت فرسنگ = 'seven farsangs' (a measure of distance), and دشت = 'plain' — 'over a plain seven farsangs (in extent).' The preposition بر marks the extent/location viewed.
segments.s387-g112: کزیشان is a compound 'کز + ایشان' meaning 'from them / from which' (i.e., 'out of that [plain]'). همی is a narrative/progressive marker ('then/so') and here links the following predicate. آسمان تیره گشت is 'the sky became dark' (آسمان = sky, تیره = dark/ominous, گشت = became). So the clause says that from that place the sky turned dark.
segments.s387-g49: A nominal phrase: همان (demonstrative 'that/same') precedes the noun as emphasis, then the title and name شاه لهراسپ. با introduces a comitative phrase ('with') and پیر سر is a terse epithet/GN 'the aged/elderly chief' (pیر 'old/elder' + سر 'head/chief'). Word order is typical Persian: determiner + noun + modifiers, and با + NP marks accompaniment.
segments.s387-g50: Main clause: همه بلخ is the subject 'the whole/entire Balkh'. ازو is an agentive/causal prepositional expression 'by him / from him'. گشت is the past/preterite of گشتن used here as 'became/was turned' (resultative/intransitive). زیر و زبر is an idiomatic paired expression 'under-and-over' = 'upside down/overturned'. The conjunction و simply links the paired adjectives.
segments.s387-g51: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of'. گرزم is 'my mace' (گرز + -م possessive). آنچ 'that which' introduces a nominal content clause, and بر ما رسید is a light-verb clause where رسید (past) means 'came upon / befell' (بر = 'upon/on' + ما = 'us'). The whole segment gives the thing being commented on: 'from my mace, that which befell us'.
segments.s387-g52: Compound negative remark: ندیدست is an older/poetic perfect/result form meaning 'has not been seen' (impersonal/passive sense). هرگز + نه (in the next verb) intensifies the negation 'never'. کسی in negative contexts functions as 'anyone/no one' and شنید is used with نه to mean 'nor has anyone heard (of it)'. The sentence contains a stacked negative: 'no one has ever seen or heard (it)'.
segments.s387-g53: بدرد من functions adverbially as 'to/for my benefit' (بدرد = 'to be of use/for the sake of'), followed by temporal اکنون 'now'. The main clause is an imperative: تو (you) + خرسند باش 'be glad' (باش = imperative). The address is direct; the verb is an exhortation.
segments.s387-g54: Another imperative clause introduced by the locative phrase به گیتی 'in the world'. درخت برومند is a metaphorical NP 'a thriving/robust tree' (درخت = tree; برومند = flourishing/prosperous). باش is again the imperative 'be!'. Word order: locative phrase, then NP predicate, then imperative verb.
segments.s387-g55: A subordinate/explanatory clause introduced by که 'that/because': من رفتنی‌ام is a predicate with the deverbal adjective رفتنی 'about to go / departing' + enclitic copula -ام 'I am' meaning 'I am going/soon to depart'. به دیگر سرای is a prepositional phrase 'to another house/abode (i.e. another world)'. The clause gives the reason for the following injunction.
segments.s387-g56: Main admonition: تو باید 'you must' uses باید plus the subjunctive-like verb باشی to express necessity/obligation (باید + که + باشی = 'you ought to be / remain'). همیشه 'always' and به جای 'in place' indicate duration and location/status. Word order places the modal باید before the complement clause introduced by که.
segments.s387-g41: بدو گفت is a short clause meaning 'he said to him': بدو is the allative/dative 'to him' and گفت is the past tense verb 'said'. کای شاه پرخاشجوی is a vocative phrase addressing the addressee: کای = 'O', شاه = 'king', پرخاشجوی = 'quarrelsome/aggressive' and it directly follows the reporting verb as the quoted addressee.
segments.s387-g42: ترا این گزند از که آمد به روی is a question 'from whom did this harm come upon you?': ترا is the second‑person marker/suffix meaning 'to/for you' (here marking the person affected), این گزند = 'this harm/injury', از که = 'from whom' (که used interrogatively), آمد = past 'came', and به روی literally 'onto the face' = 'upon you' (idiomatic: 'befell you'). Word order is object phrase + source phrase + verb + locative.
segments.s387-g43: کزو = 'from whom/from which' (poetic form of از که). کین تو = 'your grudge/vengeance' (کین with the following تو as the person concerned). باز خواهم is the verb complex: باز here has the sense 'back/again' → 'repay/avenge' and خواهم is the first‑person singular auxiliary 'I will' (future). به جنگ is a prepositional phrase 'in/into battle' specifying the manner/place of repayment.
segments.s387-g44: اگر ... اوگر is a paired conditional 'if ... or if' (اوگر is a poetic variant of اگر). شیر جنگیست = 'he is a warlike/lion of war' where جنگیست is a predicative adjective 'is warlike' (the -ست is the old 3rd‑person copula). پلنگ = 'leopard' (alternative target of the condition). The line offers two possibilities about the enemy's nature.
segments.s387-g45: چنین داد پاسخ = 'he gave an answer thus' where چنین is the manner adverb 'thus', داد is the verb 'gave' (past), and پاسخ is the object 'answer' placed after the verb (inverted order common in poetry). که‌ای پهلوان introduces direct speech with a vocative: که here links the reported speech and ای پهلوان = 'O champion'.
segments.s387-g46: ز گشتاسپم من خلیده‌روان = 'I am (one) of Gūshtāsp, secluded-souled': ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of', گشتاسپم is the name Gūshtāsp with a pronominal/attributive ending indicating 'of Gūshtāsp' or 'belonging to Gūshtāsp', من is the emphatic pronoun 'I', and خلیده‌روان is a compound adjective 'withdrawn/quiet-souled' describing the speaker's temperament.
segments.s387-g47: چو = 'when' (temporal conjunction). پای ترا = 'your foot' (پای = foot/base, ترا = the 2nd‑person marker 'your' or 'you' as object). او نکردی = 'he did not do' (او = 'he', نکردی = past negative 'did not make/do'), به بند = 'into a bond/in fetters' (بند = fetter/bond). The clause says that because he failed to bind/restrain your foot, i.e. to hold you, something followed.
segments.s387-g48: ز ترکان بما نامدی این گزند = 'this harm/misfortune came to us from the Turks': ز = 'from', ترکان = 'the Turks', بما = 'to us' (بـ + ما), نامدی = 'misfortune / calamity befell' (nominal/poetic term for harm that occurred), این گزند = 'this injury/harm' (the thing that befell). Word order places the source and recipient before the verb/nominal predicate.
segments.s387-g33: همه (object) comes before the verb for emphasis: here it means 'all/everyone'. بی‌رهان is an adjectival noun 'the unrestrained, lawless' describing that group. را is the definite-object marker attaching to همه بی‌رهان. بدین is formed from the prefix بـ (here the directional/prefix meaning 'to' or 'toward') + دین (poetic/archaic element meaning 'this' or 'here'), so the phrase means 'to this place / here'. آورم is 1st-person singular present/volitive ('I will bring' or 'I shall bring') and governs the object marked by را.
segments.s387-g34: سر جادوان is a noun phrase 'the heads of the magicians' (سر = head; جادوان = magicians/enchanters). بر زمین is a directional phrase 'onto/upon the ground' with بر = 'on/upon' and زمین = 'ground'. آورم repeats the 1st-person verb 'I will bring' and applies to this direct object; poetically it indicates bringing the magicians' heads down to the earth (i.e., laying them on the ground / executing them).
segments.s387-g35: بگفت is a past-tense narrative verb 'he said'. این 'this' refers to the just-stated words. و here links clauses ('and' / 'then'). برگاشت is a past tense verb 'he lifted/took up' used here in the sense 'mounted' or 'took up' (his horse). اسپ is the direct object 'steed/horse'. نبرد is the noun 'battle' used adverbially/goal-wise ('for battle' or 'to the fight'), so برگاشت اسپ نبرد = 'he mounted his steed for battle.'
segments.s387-g36: بیامد is the past tense 'he came'. به نزدیک is a locative phrase 'to/near' (به = to/towards; نزدیک = near). فرشیدورد is a personal name and the destination/goal of the motion. Word order is subject omitted (he) + verb + directional phrase.
segments.s387-g37: ورا is an object/pronoun 'him' (to him). از بر جامه is an archaic locative compound: از + بر + جامه literally 'from upon the garment/cloak' and introduces the position. بر خفته is a participial phrase 'lying/sleeping upon' (بر = 'on/upon', خفته = 'asleep/lying'); دید is the past verb 'he saw'. The clause orders the object (ورا) before the descriptive locative phrase and the verb, a common poetic/classical pattern.
segments.s387-g38: تن خسته is a noun phrase 'the weary body' (تن = body; خسته = weary). در جامه is a simple locative 'in/inside the garment' (در = in; جامه = garment). بنهفته is a perfective/adjectival form 'hidden, tucked in' (بـ prefix + نهفته). دید repeats the past verb 'he saw.' The second clause restates and zooms in: he saw the tired body concealed within the robe.
segments.s387-g39: ز (poetic from) introduces source 'from the eye'. دیده = 'eye'. ببارید is a past-tense verb 'fell' or 'rained down' used figuratively for tears. چنان is an intensifier 'so much/so many'; سرشک means 'teardrop(s)'. The clause is an idiomatic classical construction: 'From the eye fell so many teardrops...'.
segments.s387-g40: که introduces a result/consequence clause 'that...'. با = 'with' and درد او = 'his pain/suffering' (درد = pain; او = he). آشنا شد is a compound meaning 'became acquainted/aware' (آشنا = familiar; شد = became). پزشک (physician) is the subject/agent that becomes acquainted; classical word order puts the verb before the nominative noun here, so 'that the physician became acquainted with his pain.'
segments.s387-g57: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning 'when'. رفتم is simple past first-person 'I went/left' (here 'I leave/shall leave' in a prophetic/poetic sense). ز گیتی is a prepositional phrase 'from the world' (ز = 'from', گیتی = 'world'). مرا یاددار is an imperative construction: مرا = 'me' with the object marker را, and یاددار is a poetic/archaic imperative 'keep in memory / remember'.
segments.s387-g58: به بخشش is an ablative/instrumental phrase 'by/with (your) forgiveness' (به used here as 'by/with'). روان مرا is 'my soul' (روان = soul; مرا = 'me' as object-form, here marking the possessor construction in older style). شاددار is an optative/imperative-like verb 'make (it) joyful' or 'keep (it) joyful'—a poetic command/request.
segments.s387-g59: تو is the vocative/second‑person pronoun 'you'. پدرود باش is an imperative/final formula: پدرود = 'farewell' and باش is the imperative 'be' (here 'be farewell' or more idiomatically 'farewell to you'). ای جهان‌پهلوان is a vocative epithet 'O world‑champion / mighty hero'.
segments.s387-g60: که introduces a wished-for clause ('that' / 'may it be that'). جاوید is the adjective 'eternal'. بادی here is an archaic/poetic verbal form marking the optative ('may you be'); its lexical senses listed in the data (relating to 'wind') do not match this verbal use. و روشن‌روان links a second wish 'and bright‑souled' (روشن‌روان = 'bright‑soul' / 'clear‑souled').
segments.s387-g61: بگفت این = 'he said this' (بگفت = he said). و links the next action: رخسارگان کرد زرد = 'he caused the countenances/faces to turn pale' (کرد is causative 'made/caused', رخسارگان = faces, زرد = pale). Word order places the adjective after the verb in this poetic line.
segments.s387-g62: شد is the past/inchoative 'became' (here functioning as the copular/Result verb). آن نامور شاه فرشیدورد is the noun phrase 'that renowned king, Farshidvard' in apposition; the clause thus says that the named king became (pale/affected) as described previously.
segments.s387-g63: بزد is simple past 'he struck' or 'he smote'. دست is the object 'hand' and بر جامه اسفندیار is a prepositional phrase 'upon Esfandiyar's garment/robe' (بر = on/upon, جامه = robe, اسفندیار = proper name). The construction describes a physical gesture.
segments.s387-g64: همه پرنیان = 'all the silken (robes/cloths)' (همه = entire/all; پرنیان = silken robe/cloth). بر تنش = 'upon his body' (بر = on, تن = body, ش = enclitic 'his'). گشت is 'became/turned' and خار is the predicate 'thorn(s)' — so 'all the silk on his body turned to thorns.'
segments.s387-g9: ازان باره is a temporal/discourse adverbial 'on that occasion / thereupon.' دژ is the place 'the fortress.' چو is the conjunction 'when.' بیرون شد(ند) is a classical/periphrastic expression meaning 'came out / issued forth' — شد(ند) is the past of شدن used here to mark the change of state / exit (poetic usage for 'went out'). Word order: temporal phrase + place + 'when' + verb phrase.
segments.s387-g10: سواران جنگی is the subject ('the war-horsemen' / 'armed riders'), with جنگی as an adjectival epithet 'warlike' / 'military.' به + هامون marks direction/place 'to/onto the plain.' شدند again marks the action in past (they went/rode), used poetically for movement/arrival.
segments.s387-g11: سپهبد is the subject (the commander). سوی آسمان is a directional phrase 'toward heaven.' کرد + روی is an idiomatic sequence: کرد (make/do) with روی ('face') yields 'turned his face' — a common classical Persian causative/putting-construction meaning 'he turned (his) face toward...'.
segments.s387-g12: چنین گفت = 'thus he said' (چنین 'thus', گفت past 'said'). کای is a vocative particle 'O', here addressing داور راست‌گوی 'the truthful judge' — a short vocative phrase beginning the direct speech. Note راست‌گوی is an epithet attached to داور.
segments.s387-g13: توی here is an archaic/prepositional/vocative 'thou' / 'O' + locution addressing the addressee; آفریننده و کامگار are two coordinated epithets 'Creator and accomplisher / fulfiller.' The phrase is vocative/appositional, addressing the divine agent.
segments.s387-g14: فروزنده is a participial epithet 'one who lights/brightens' or 'illuminator'; جان is 'life' (object of فروزنده) and اسفندیار is the name in genitive relation. Together this is an appositive epithet: 'illuminator of Esfandiyar's life.'
segments.s387-g15: تو دانی که = 'you know that' (تو 'you', دانی present/poetic 'know', که introducing the clause). از خون فرشیدورد is a prepositional phrase 'from/from the blood of Farshidvard' giving the cause or circumstance; word order is typical: verb 'know' followed by the subordinate clause.
segments.s387-g16: دلم is دل + possessive suffix -م 'my heart.' گشت is the past of گشتن/شدن 'became' (my heart became). پر + درد = 'full of pain' (پر = 'full (of)'). The coordinate phrase و رخساره زرد is a verbless clause: 'and (my) complexion (was) pale' — the verb is omitted but understood (typical in poetry), so رخساره زرد = 'the face/complexion turned pale.'
segments.s387-g81: برآهیخت is a poetic past 3rd-person singular meaning 'rose up' or 'was roused' and here is the main verb. خفتان جنگ is a noun-phrase 'the slumber (خفتان) of battle (جنگ)' placed after the verb (typical in poetry), and از تنش is a prepositional phrase ('from his body') with the possessive enclitic -ش attached to تن. The whole segment reads literally 'the slumber of battle rose from his body.'
segments.s387-g82: کفن کرد is a light-verb construction: کفن ('shroud') plus کرد ('did/made') meaning 'made into a shroud' or 'shrouded/wrapped.' دستار و پیراهنش are the coordinated objects ('turban and his tunic'); the possessive enclitic -ش appears on the final noun and in Persian can be understood as applying to the whole coordinated noun phrase.
segments.s387-g83: وزانجا is a fused adverbial 'and/from there' (وز = and + انجا = there). بیامد is past tense 'he came.' ب + دان form the prepositional phrase بدان 'to that,' followed by جایگاه 'place'; together بدان جایگاه = 'to that place.'
segments.s387-g84: کجا here introduces a relative/location clause 'where.' شاه گشتاسپ names the subject 'King Gushtasp.' گم کرد راه is a verbal phrase where گم کرد (literally 'made lost') functions as 'lost' with راه ('way/path') as the object — a common Persian expression meaning 'lost his way.' Word order is roughly 'where king Gushtasp lost the way.'
segments.s387-g85: بسی here functions as an intensive quantifier 'many' (poetic usage). مرد ز ایرانیان کشته دید is literally 'he saw many men of the Iranians slain' — کشته is a postpositive adjective/participial form 'slain' modifying مرد, and دید is the past tense 'he saw.'
segments.s387-g86: شده is a perfect/passive participle 'had become' or 'were become.' خاک و ریگ 'earth and sand' are coordinated nouns, and از جهان ناپدید means 'vanished from the world' with ناپدید as the adjective/verb 'vanished.' The clause reads 'they had become dust and sand, vanished from the world.'
segments.s387-g87: همی is an adverb often meaning 'continually' or giving emphasis; زار here is an adverbial/adjunct meaning 'in lament' or 'woefully.' بگریست is the past tense 'he wept/wailed.' بر کشتگان is the prepositional object 'over the slain.' Together: 'he wailed woefully over the slain.'
segments.s387-g88: پر از درد دل شد is a common copular structure: پر از درد 'full of pain' + دل شد 'his heart became.' ازان خستگان is an ablative/cause phrase 'because of/from those weary ones' (ازان = 'from/of those'), so the whole line means 'his heart became full of sorrow/pain because of those exhausted/weary ones.'
segments.s387-g113: This clause begins with the indefinite/identifying 'یکی' (a certain one) as subject, followed by the compound participial construction 'کنده کرده' (literally 'having dug' / 'had dug'), with the directional phrase 'به گرد اندرون' ('around the inside') placed after it. Word order is Subject + perfect-participial verb phrase + prepositional/location phrase; 'کنده کرده' uses the past participle + 'کرده' to express a completed action in archaic/epic register.
segments.s387-g114: This nominal phrase with measure describes the extent of the digging: 'به پهنای' = 'to a breadth of', followed by 'پرتاب تیری فزون' where 'پرتابِ تیری' ('an arrow's projection/flight') is qualified by the adjective 'فزون' ('greater/more than'). The preposition 'به' introduces the measure, and the adjective comes at the end in typical Persian order.
segments.s387-g115: The clause begins with the poetic preposition 'ز' ('from'), indicating origin ('from the dug place'), then 'کنده' (the dug/dug-out place) and the expression 'به صد چاره اندر گذشت' where 'به' means 'by/with', 'صد چاره' = 'a hundred expedients/stratagems', and 'اندر گذشت' is a past verb phrase 'passed/ slipped through into'. The sequence shows source + instrument/means + motion verb (he passed through using many tricks).
segments.s387-g116: This clause gives the rider's next action: 'عنان را نهاده' is a prefixed object-marker 'را' on 'عنان' (the reins) with 'نهاده' (placed/laid) meaning 'having laid/set (the reins)', followed by the directional phrase 'بران سوی دشت' ('toward that side of the plain'). The participial/absolute sense 'نهاده' marks a concomitant action (he set the reins, then went toward that side).
segments.s387-g117: 'طلایه' (vanguard) is the subject followed by the poetic preposition 'ز' ('of/from') marking origin ('of the Turks'), then 'چو هشتاد مرد' ('like/as eighty men') where 'چو' functions as 'about/like' to give an approximate number. Word order: subject — genitive phrase — simile/approximate numeral.
segments.s387-g118: This predicate uses the old/poetic continuous particle 'همی' + past 'گشت' to indicate repeated or ongoing action ('kept going/was going'), followed by the locative complex 'بر گرد دشت' ('around the plain') and the noun 'نبرد' (battle/fighting) to show their activity. The sequence reads: they kept going around the plain, engaged in battle/fighting.
segments.s387-g119: 'برآهیخت' is an elevated/epic past form meaning 'he lifted up / he was roused', directly followed by its object 'شمشیر' (sword). The conjunction 'و' links to the next action; 'اندر نهاد' is an idiomatic sequence meaning 'placed/settled within' (i.e., inwardly fixed or set in his heart). The line thus pairs an outward physical motion (lifting the sword) with an inward placement.
segments.s387-g120: Starts with the continuous particle 'همی' + verb 'کرد' ('made/did') used here to mean 'he caused/kept in mind', followed by the preposition 'از' ('of/regarding') and the genitive phrase 'رزم گشتاسپ یاد' ('the memory/remembrance of Gushtasp's battle'). The whole phrase means he inwardly called to mind or fixed the remembrance of Gushtasp's combat.
segments.s387-g97: Relative clause introducing a general statement: که (that/who) marks 'an enemy who...'. The verb بود is the past copula (here used proverbially as a generalizing past), producing 'an enemy who was/is wise'. The comparative phrase begins with به ز (به + ز) meaning 'better than', where ز functions as the poetic 'than' and دوست is the standard noun 'friend'. Word order is literary (VSO/subject following verb and relative clauses).
segments.s387-g98: ابا here denotes 'reluctance/refusal' or the trait 'being unwilling', applied to a friend (دوست) and contrasted with a wise enemy; the coordinating و links دشمن and دوست. پایان جمله (دانش نکوست) states the evaluative conclusion 'knowledge is good' — دانش (knowledge) with the copula نکوست 'is good' (poetic adjectival predicate). The syntax is compact and proverbial: 'a reluctant friend (اباِ دوست) is not as good; knowing who is enemy and who is friend is good.'
segments.s387-g99: براندیشد is a literary third-person verb 'to think/consider' (V before subject, typical in poetry); it can be read as general/prescriptive 'let/one who thinks' or simply 'he thinks'. آنکس که دانا بود is a relative nominal phrase 'the one who was/is wise' with بود as the past copula used generically. The clause thus means 'the one who is wise thinks/considers...'.
segments.s387-g100: به کاری = 'about/over a task' (به introducing the object matter). که introduces a subordinate relative clause 'that' (which task) and بر وی توانا بود is 'was able upon him' i.e. 'that he was capable of' — بر وی (on him) is the classical way to mark the one affected, and توانا بود is the copular expression 'was able/capable'. Word order mirrors classical Persian: relative clause after the head noun (کاری).
segments.s387-g101: ز چیزی که افتد بران ناتوان is a relative clause 'about something which befalls that weak/helpless one'. ز is the poetic 'about/of' (introducing the thing), چیزی 'something', که 'which/that', افتد is a present/jussive-form meaning 'befalls/comes to' (classical usage), بران 'upon that one' (بران = بر + آن), and ناتوان 'the incapable/weak' describes the affected person.
segments.s387-g102: به جستنش = 'in seeking it' (به + جستن with enclitic object -ش). رنجه ندارد روان is an idiomatic clause: رنجه (suffering/distress) + ندارد 'does not have' yields 'does not suffer' or 'does not trouble himself'; روان here means 'spirit/soul' and is the patient/possessor of that non-suffering. Together: 'his spirit does not suffer in seeking it' — i.e. one does not weary himself searching after what befell the weak.
segments.s387-g103: از ایران همی جای من خواستی is literal: از ایران 'from Iran' (indicating domain or origin), همی is a poetic particle (here emphatic: 'indeed/just'), جای من 'my place/position', and خواستی is second-person past 'you wanted/you sought'. The object (my place in/of Iran) follows the particle and verb in typical Persian order.
segments.s387-g104: برافگندی is a second-person past 'you cast/you threw down' (بـ + افگندی), اندر جهان 'in the world' (اندر = in/within), and کاستی is a noun 'diminution/deficiency' (here the result brought into the world by the action). Grammatically the verb predicates the action and the following noun names the resulting lack or diminution caused.
segments.s387-g73: ز is the archaic poetic preposition 'from' (like modern از). هامون is a geographic noun 'the plain'; بیامد is a past-form verb 'he came' (classical/poetic verbal form meaning ‘came’). به + کوه بلند is a goal phrase 'to the high mountain' (به = to/towards, adjective بلند follows the noun).
segments.s387-g74: برادرش = 'his brother' (برادر + enclitic -ش 'his'). بسته is a participial/adjectival use 'bound, tied' describing the brother. بر introduces the location 'on/upon' and اسپ سمند is the noun phrase 'the steed Samand' (سمند here is the specific steed/horse).
segments.s387-g75: همی گفت: همی before a past verb (گفت) gives a habitual/iterative or heightened narrative tone ('he said' / 'he kept saying') in classical Persian. کاکنون (کـ + اکنون) = 'now, at this moment' (archaic compound). چه سازم ترا: چه is the interrogative 'what', سازم is 1st-person present/subjunctive/optative of ساختن 'shall I do/make', and ترا is the object 'you' written as the 2nd-person suffix ت plus the object marker را (here separated), so the whole clause = 'what shall I do for you?'.
segments.s387-g76: یکی دخمه = 'one/a certain tomb' (یکی as an indefinite article). چون here introduces the clause that follows ('as/that/so that'); برفرازم is an older verbal form meaning 'I will raise/exalt (it or you)'—functioning as a purposive/optative relative to دخمه (i.e. 'a tomb which I will raise up'). ترا again is the object 'you'. (In classical phrasing the relative/purpose clause may follow the noun.)
segments.s387-g77: نه ... نه ... is the standard paired negative 'neither ... nor ...'. نه چیزست با من = 'there is nothing with me' where چیزست is a predicate 'there is/it is a thing' used here in negative. The following نه سیم و نه زر lists valuables 'neither silver nor gold'.
segments.s387-g78: Continuation of the negative list: نه خشت و نه آب و نه دیوارگر = 'neither mudbrick nor water nor a mason (builder)'. Each noun is coordinated by repeated نه to stress lack of each item or person.
segments.s387-g79: به زیر درختی = 'underneath a tree' (به + زیر = 'beneath'; درختی = 'a tree'). که introduces a relative clause 'which/that'; بد سایه‌دار is an adjectival phrase. In classical usage بد here functions idiomatically (not strictly 'bad') to qualify سایه‌دار; in context it means 'richly/well shaded' (a common poetic collocation), so the clause describes a tree giving substantial shade.
segments.s387-g80: نهادش is the past form of نهادن with the object suffix -ش 'him' = 'he placed him'. بدان is the fused deictic/prepositional sense 'in/into that' (here written as ب + دان), and جایگه نامدار = 'that place, renowned' (جایگه 'place'; نامدار 'famous/illustrious'). The whole clause = 'he placed him in that famed spot.'
segments.s387-g89: This is a poetic, somewhat inverted clause meaning 'they had fought to what place' (i.e. 'they had gone off to fight somewhere'). The phrase به جایی کجا = 'to what place / to somewhere' precedes the verbal complex کرده بودند, where کرده is the perfect participle and بودند is the past auxiliary forming a pluperfect sense ('had done/fought'). رزم is a verbal noun meaning 'battle/war' and functions as the object/theme of کرده بودند.
segments.s387-g90: به چشم آمدش is an idiomatic expression 'it came to his eye' = 'it appeared to him / he saw'. آمدش is آمد + enclitic -ش 'to him'. زرد روی is a two-word adjectival phrase 'yellow-faced / pale-faced' modifying the following noun. گرزم here functions as the noun that appeared (likely a proper name or a noun 'mace' used as a name); word order places the verb phrase before the descriptive phrase for poetic effect.
segments.s387-g91: به نزدیک او is a locative prepositional phrase 'near him'. اسپش is اسپ + enclitic -ش 'his steed/horse' (the possesive clitic fused to the noun). افگنده بود is a participle (افگنده = 'cast/thrown down') plus the past auxiliary بود, so 'was thrown down / lay cast down' describing the steed's state.
segments.s387-g92: This clause describes the scene around him. خاک is the subject 'dust/earth', پراگنده بود is the predicate 'was scattered', and چندی is an adverbial 'for a while / for some time'. برو here functions as a locative/relational particle in the phrase (poetic usage equivalent to 'around/on/near'), so the whole means 'around/on the ground dust for a time was scattered.'
segments.s387-g93: چنین گفت is 'thus he said/spoke'. با marks the addressee 'to/with' and is followed by کشته اسفندیار 'the slain Esfandiyar' — کشته is used as a substantive 'the slain (one)'. The clause means 'thus he spoke to the slain Esfandiyar.'
segments.s387-g94: که‌ای here functions as a poetic vocative (roughly 'O' or 'O you'), followed by مرد نادان 'ignorant man' (مرد = man/person/hero, نادان = ignorant/foolish). بد روزگار is a compound epithet 'ill-fated' or 'of bad fortune' where بد modifies روزگار (fate/fortune). The phrase is an address: 'O ignorant man, ill-fated one.'
segments.s387-g95: نگه کن is an imperative 'look!' or 'pay attention!'. که introduces a subordinate clause 'that / what', and دانای ایران is the subject 'the wise man (sage) of Iran'. چه گفت is a fused interrogative/exclamatory phrase 'what he said' (چه = what, گفت = said). The whole segment = 'Look at what the sage of Iran said.'
segments.s387-g96: بدانگه 'thereupon' is a discourse adverb linking to the previous action. که again introduces the clause; بگشاد is a past verb 'he opened/revealed' (ب- + گشاد), راز is the object 'secret', and از نهفت is a prepositional phrase 'from what was hidden' (نهفت = hidden/concealed). Together: 'thereupon, when he revealed the secret that had been hidden.'
segments.s387-g65: همی گفت is a classical/perhaps poetic imperfective/perfective-stative form: همی before a past verb adds an ongoing or habitual sense (here rendered simply as 'said'). کای is the vocative particle 'O'. پاک برتر are epithets placed before the noun (adjectives precede the noun in poetry here) and خدای is the vocative noun 'God' (here addressed directly).
segments.s387-g66: به نیکی is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'in/with goodness'. تو باشی is the 2nd-person singular subjunctive/optative of باش (باشی) used as a wish 'may you be'. مرا رهنمای is a predicative construction where مرا ('me' as object/beneficiary) precedes the noun رهنمای ('guide'); together with باشی it means 'be my guide' (literally 'you be guide for me').
segments.s387-g67: که introduces a subordinate/purpose clause here ('so that' / 'that'). پیش آورم combines the adverbial پیش 'before/in the presence of' with آورم (1st-person singular present/subjunctive of آوردن) 'I may bring/bring before'. کین is the noun 'vengeance/hatred' acting as the object being brought; فرشیدورد is the proper name in apposition/genitive relation ('of Farshidvard').
segments.s387-g68: برانگیزم is 1st-person singular of برانگیختن (here subjunctive/intentional) 'I will rouse/raise'. از is the preposition 'from/out of'. رود is 'river' (often collective). وز in classical/poetic spelling functions as a contracted 'و ز' ('and from') or a connective; کوه گرد is a short phrase where کوه 'mountain' combines with گرد ('around' / 'encircling') to mean 'the surrounding mountains' or 'mountain‑regions' — overall 'I will rouse (him/them) from rivers and the encircling mountains.'
segments.s387-g69: بریزم is a first‑person singular present/subjunctive (poetic) verb meaning “I will pour/spill.” ز تن is a prepositional phrase ‘from the body’ (ز = from + تن = body) that modifies خون; خون ارجاسپ را is the definite object ‘the blood of Arjasp’ marked by را. Word order is poetic: verb first, then source phrase, then the object with را before the verb’s completion.
segments.s387-g70: شکیبا here is an adjective meaning ‘steadfast/enduring’ used as a resultative/complement; کنم is the first‑person singular of کردن ‘to make/cause’ (I will make); جان لهراسپ را is the object ‘the life/soul of Lohrasp’ with را marking it as definite. Roughly: “I will cause Lohrasp’s life to be steadfast.”
segments.s387-g71: برادرش = ‘his brother’ (برادر + enclitic ش = his). The clitic را marks that phrase as a definite object. مرده is a post‑nominal adjective ‘dead.’ بر زین نهاد uses نهاد (past of نهادن ‘to place’) as the main verb, with بر زین ‘on the saddle’ as the locative phrase. Literal order: ‘his brother (object), dead, upon the saddle he placed.’
segments.s387-g72: This is a pair of adjectival phrases linked by parataxis: دلی پر ز کینه = ‘a heart full of hatred’ (دلی = heart, پر ز = full of, کینه = hatred). لبی پر ز باد = ‘a lip/mouth full of wind’ where باد (literally ‘wind’) is used metaphorically for empty or boastful words; the same construction (لبی + پر ز + باد) describes his speech. The phrases are descriptive complements characterizing the subject.
segments.s387-g25: به گیتی is a prepositional phrase placed up front meaning 'in/throughout the world' (به + گیتی). صد آتشکده نو کنم is a verb phrase with صد (hundred) + noun آتشکده (fire-temples) + adjective نو (new) and the verb کنم (1st-person 'I make/do'). Here کنم is the classical present/subjunctive used to express the speaker's intention/volition: 'I will make/build a hundred new fire-temples.'
segments.s387-g26: This clause shares the same subject and verb sense (کنم) as the previous clause. جهان is the patient/affected domain 'the world'; از + ستمگاره here marks separation or removal 'from/away from oppressors'; بی‌خو is a compound adjective (بی- 'without' + خو 'sleep') used idiomatically to mean 'deprived of/without (oppressors)' or more loosely 'rid of tyrants'. The verb کنم again expresses the speaker's determined intention: 'I will make the world free of oppressors.'
segments.s387-g27: نبیند is the negative present/subjunctive (ن + بیند from دیدن) giving a jussive/optative sense 'may no one see' or 'let no one see.' کسی here functions as 'anyone/no one' in this negative context. پای من بر بساط is the prepositional complement 'my foot on the carpet' (بر = on, بساط = carpet or courtly carpet/metaphor for court). The whole segment means 'let no one see my foot upon the carpet (i.e., I will not appear in courtly splendour).'
segments.s387-g28: مگر introduces an exception/contrast here ('except' or 'save that'). در بیابان = 'in the desert/wilderness.' کنم is again the 1st-person volitional/subjunctive 'I will make/build' and صد رباط is the direct object 'a hundred caravanserais/inns' (رباط = roadside inn/caravanserai). The clause contrasts public courtly life with building remote inns: 'except that in the desert I will build a hundred caravanserais.'
segments.s387-g29: به شاخی = 'on/onto a branch' (به + شاخی). که introduces a relative clause 'that/where.' کرگس is the subject 'vulture.' برو نگذرد should be read here as classical 'بر او نگذرد' (بر + او contracted) meaning 'passes over it'; نگذرد is the negative present/subjunctive/relative 'does not pass.' So the relative clause means 'on a branch over which not even a vulture passes.'
segments.s387-g30: بدو here is 'to it/unto it' (به + او contracted) referring back to that branch. گور و نخچیر is a coordinated pair 'grave (گور) and prey/booty (نخچیر)'. پی نسپرد is a verbal phrase with پی 'after/in pursuit' + نسپرد (ن + سپرد, 'did not entrust/hand over' or 'was not given'). Together the clause says that 'to it (the branch) neither burial nor spoil was handed/left' — i.e. it is so remote/unseen that no corpse nor booty is brought/left there.
segments.s387-g31: کنم is the speaker's volitional 'I will do/make.' چاه آب اندرو = 'a water-well there/in that place' (چاه = well, آب = water, اندرو = classical/inward form 'in it/there'). صدهزار follows as the numeral 'a hundred thousand' that connects to the next verb phrase; the line is compact/poetic so clauses and numerals may be distributed across the line.
segments.s387-g32: توانگر کنم is 'I will make (them) wealthy' (توانگر here functions as resultative adjective or noun 'the wealthy'), with کنم again expressing volition. مردم خیش کار is a nominal phrase 'the people (مردم) plough‑workers (خیش‌کار: those who work the plough)'; syntactically the poet lists results: 'I will make a hundred thousand rich, and the people ploughers/tillers.' Word order is terse and typical of epic Persian: adjective/nominal complement + کنم + affected group.

sentences.s387-s61: He cast many of them onto the road, and from that spot went up close to the king.
sentences.s387-s9: If ever I become victorious in battle, I will make the whole world's face hostile to Arjasp.
sentences.s387-s10: Let us exact from him, as vengeance for King Lohrasp, that same revenge for those many innocent heads.
sentences.s387-s11: My thirty-eight world-seeing brothers, whose blood turned the plain's soil into rubies.
sentences.s387-s12: I accepted from the just judge that I should not take vengeance on account of my father's imprisonment.
sentences.s387-s1: When night fell, like the vengeful Ahriman, the peal of the bell rose from the palace.
sentences.s387-s2: On that occasion someone sat down at his side; one had taken an Indian sword in his hand.
sentences.s387-s3: When Nushāzar and Bahman and Mehrnush set out, they went off all at once, full of war and ardor.
sentences.s387-s4: There was a guide for him before Jamasp, who was the minister of the auspicious Gushtasp.
sentences.s387-s53: You took the splendor from this kingship; you sought a remedy — he spoke a lie.
sentences.s387-s54: In this bloody battle that was spilled, you remain hung upon that world.
sentences.s387-s55: From that weeping plain he drew inward; he reached the dense ranks of the Turks.
sentences.s387-s56: The army saw, over a plain seven farsangs long, from which the sky grew dark.
sentences.s387-s25: That same King Lohrasp, with the aged chief at his side, turned all of Balkh upside down.
sentences.s387-s26: Of the blows of my mace — what befell us — no one has ever seen or heard (the like).
sentences.s387-s27: For my sake now, be glad, and be a flourishing tree in the world.
sentences.s387-s28: For I am about to go to another abode; you must always remain in your place.
sentences.s387-s21: He said to him, "O quarrelsome king, from whom did this injury come upon you?"
sentences.s387-s22: I will repay/avenge your grievance in battle — whether he is a warlike lion or a leopard.
sentences.s387-s23: He answered thus: "O champion — I am of Gushtasp, a man of secluded / contemplative soul."
sentences.s387-s24: When he did not bind your foot with fetters, this harm came to us from the Turks.
sentences.s387-s17: I will bring all the lawless ones here, and I will lay the magicians' heads upon the ground.
sentences.s387-s18: He said this, mounted his steed for battle, and came up close to Farshidvard.
sentences.s387-s19: He saw him lying on his cloak, and he saw the weary body hidden within the garment.
sentences.s387-s20: So many teardrops fell from his eyes that the physician became acquainted with his suffering.
sentences.s387-s29: When I have gone from the world, remember me; by your forgiveness keep my soul joyful.
sentences.s387-s30: Farewell to you, O mighty hero; may you be eternal and bright‑souled.
sentences.s387-s31: He said this, and the faces grew pale; that renowned king Farshidvard became (pale).
sentences.s387-s32: He struck his hand upon Esfandiyar’s robe; all the silken cloths upon his body turned into thorns.
sentences.s387-s5: On that occasion, when they issued from the fortress, the war-horsemen rode out onto the plain.
sentences.s387-s6: The spahbed turned his face toward heaven and thus spoke: 'O truthful Judge,'
sentences.s387-s7: O Creator and fulfiller, illuminator of Esfandiyar's life.
sentences.s387-s8: You know that from Farshidvard's blood my heart became full of pain and my complexion turned pale.
sentences.s387-s41: The slumber of battle rose from his body; he wrapped his turban and tunic as a shroud.
sentences.s387-s42: And from there he came to that place where King Gushtasp had lost his way.
sentences.s387-s43: He saw many men of the Iranians slain; they had become dust and sand, vanished from the world.
sentences.s387-s44: He wailed over the slain; his heart became full of pain because of those weary ones.
sentences.s387-s57: A certain one had dug around the interior, to a breadth exceeding the range of an arrow.
sentences.s387-s58: From the dug place he slipped through by a hundred contrivances; having set the reins, he turned toward that side of the plain.
sentences.s387-s59: The Turks' vanguard, like eighty men, went about the plain fighting.
sentences.s387-s60: He raised his sword, and inwardly fixed in himself the memory of Gushtasp's battle.
sentences.s387-s49: An enemy who is wise is better than a friend who refuses; knowledge of enemy and friend is good.
sentences.s387-s50: He who is wise will reflect on the task that he is able to perform.
sentences.s387-s51: One does not weary his spirit in seeking after something that has befallen the powerless.
sentences.s387-s52: You sought my place in Iran; you cast me down and brought diminishment into the world.
sentences.s387-s37: He came from the plain up to the high mountain; his brother was tied upon the steed Samand.
sentences.s387-s38: He said, 'And now what shall I do for you? Shall I make for you a tomb and raise it/s you up?'
sentences.s387-s39: There is nothing with me — neither silver nor gold; neither brick nor water nor a mason.
sentences.s387-s40: He placed him beneath a tree that afforded good shade, setting him in that renowned spot.
sentences.s387-s45: They had gone to fight somewhere; a pale-faced Garzam came into his sight.
sentences.s387-s46: Near him his steed lay thrown down, and for a while dust was scattered on the ground.
sentences.s387-s47: Thus he spoke to the slain Esfandiyar: 'O ignorant man, ill-fated one.'
sentences.s387-s48: Look (attend) to what the sage of Iran said thereupon, when he revealed the hidden secret.
sentences.s387-s33: He said, "O pure, supreme God, may you, in goodness, be my guide."
sentences.s387-s34: That I may bring before you this vengeance of Farshidvard, I will rouse (them/him) from rivers and the encircling mountains.
sentences.s387-s35: I will spill Arjasp’s blood from his body, and I will make Lohrasp’s life steady/enduring.
sentences.s387-s36: He placed his brother, dead, on the saddle; his heart full of hatred, his lips full of empty words.
sentences.s387-s13: I will build a hundred new fire-temples in the world, and I will rid the world of oppressive rulers.
sentences.s387-s14: Let no one see my foot upon the carpet; except that in the desert I will build a hundred caravanserais.
sentences.s387-s15: On a branch where not even a vulture passes over, to it neither grave nor prey will be entrusted.
sentences.s387-s16: I will dig a well of water there; I will make a hundred thousand rich, and make the people tillers with the plough.
